I've managed renewable energy projects from early development through commercial operation. Solar. Wind. Battery storage. And I can tell you that the single biggest threat to the clean energy transition right now isn't technology. It isn't cost. It isn't even politics.

It's the interconnection queue.

> There are currently over 2,600 GW of generation and storage capacity waiting in US interconnection queues. That's more than double the entire installed generating capacity of the United States. Let that number sink in for a moment.

You can build the cheapest solar farm in history. You can have your financing locked, your permits approved, your equipment ordered. And then you submit your interconnection application -- and wait. And wait. And wait some more. Five years, on average, from application to commercial operation. Some projects wait longer. Many never connect at all.

This is the interconnection queue crisis. And if you're working in clean energy, it's the bottleneck that should keep you up at night.

---

## What Is the Interconnection Queue, and Why Should You Care?

For those who aren't neck-deep in grid development, here's the short version.

Before any power plant -- solar, wind, gas, nuclear, anything -- can connect to the electrical grid, it needs to go through an interconnection study process. The grid operator needs to verify that your project won't destabilize the transmission system, that the local infrastructure can handle the additional power flow, and that reliability standards are maintained.

That makes sense. You don't want someone plugging a 500 MW solar farm into a transmission line that can't handle it. Safety first.

The problem is that this study process was designed decades ago for a handful of large, centralized power plants. Maybe a few dozen applications per year in a given region. The studies were sequential, thorough, and slow -- but manageable.

Then the clean energy revolution happened. Solar costs dropped 90%. Battery storage became economically viable. Wind became the cheapest source of electricity in many regions. And suddenly, instead of a few dozen applications per year, grid operators started receiving thousands.

According to the Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory's Queued Up report (2025), over 11,000 projects are actively sitting in US interconnection queues right now. The total capacity waiting -- generation plus storage -- exceeds 2,600 GW. To put that in perspective, the entire US grid has roughly 1,250 GW of installed capacity.

> We have more than twice the grid's current capacity just sitting in line, waiting for permission to connect. The queue isn't a bottleneck anymore. It's a wall.

---

## Five Years Is the New Normal

Here's a number that should alarm anyone working in this industry: the average time from interconnection request to commercial operation in the US is now approximately five years (LBNL Grid Connection Study 2025). That's up from roughly three years a decade ago.

Five years. Think about what that means in practice.

- Your project economics are based on today's power purchase agreement prices and today's equipment costs. Five years from now, both could look completely different.
- Your financing has a shelf life. Lenders and investors don't wait around forever.
- Your land lease payments are ticking from day one. Five years of lease payments with zero revenue is a brutal cash burn.
- Policy incentives like the ITC and PTC have phase-down schedules. A project that was economically viable when you applied might not be when you finally get permission to connect.

From a PM perspective, this is a scheduling nightmare. You can manage every other variable on your project to perfection, and the interconnection timeline will still be the critical path by a massive margin. It's the one thing you can't accelerate with better execution.

---

## Why the Queue Is Broken

I've spent enough time in this industry to understand that the problem isn't malice or incompetence. The grid operators are doing their best with a process that was never designed for this volume. But understanding the root causes matters if we're going to fix it.

**The serial study problem.** Traditionally, interconnection studies were conducted one project at a time, in the order received. Each study assumes that every project ahead of it in the queue will get built. So if you're project number 500, your study models the grid impact of all 499 projects before you -- most of which will never actually get built. The result is wildly inflated cost estimates for network upgrades, because you're paying for a fictional version of the grid that will never exist.

**Speculative applications.** Because there's no penalty for submitting an application that you're not serious about, developers have been flooding queues with speculative projects. Apply for ten sites, see which one gets favorable study results, and abandon the rest. This clogs the queue with phantom projects that waste everyone's time and money.

**Understaffed grid operators.** The transmission providers processing these studies are drowning. Their engineering teams were sized for a world with a few hundred applications per year. Now they're getting thousands. FERC and regional ISOs have acknowledged the staffing shortfall, but hiring and training transmission planning engineers takes time.

**Cascading restudies.** When a project ahead of you in the queue withdraws -- and as I'll explain, most of them do -- every project behind it often needs to be restudied, because the grid conditions have changed. This creates a vicious cycle: withdrawals trigger restudies, restudies take more time, more time leads to more withdrawals, which trigger more restudies.

> The interconnection queue wasn't designed to break. But it also wasn't designed for a world where solar and storage are the cheapest forms of new electricity and everyone wants to build them simultaneously.

---

## The Human Cost: Millions Spent on Projects That May Never Exist

Let's talk about money, because this is where the crisis gets personal for developers.

To enter the interconnection queue, you need to post study deposits. These aren't trivial amounts. Depending on the region and project size, developers are putting up anywhere from $50,000 to several million dollars just to get their project studied. And if the studies reveal that your project would require significant network upgrades -- which is increasingly common as queues get longer -- you could be looking at tens of millions in assigned upgrade costs.

I've seen developers commit $2-3 million in interconnection deposits and study costs for a single project, only to discover two years later that their assigned network upgrade costs make the project economically unviable. That money is largely gone.

For smaller developers and independent power producers, this is existential. The interconnection queue has become a de facto barrier to entry that favors only the largest, most well-capitalized companies -- the ones that can afford to play a numbers game with dozens of applications, absorbing losses on the majority.

That's not how a healthy market should work.

---

## 80% of Projects Never Get Built

This is the statistic that really captures the dysfunction: more than 80% of projects that enter interconnection queues ultimately withdraw without ever being built (LBNL Queued Up 2025).

Let me repeat that. Four out of five projects in the queue will never generate a single electron.

Some withdraw because their study results came back with prohibitive upgrade costs. Some withdraw because the timeline stretched so long that their financing fell apart. Some were speculative from the start. And some withdraw because their PPA expired while they were waiting, or because equipment prices shifted, or because the political landscape changed.

Whatever the reason, the result is the same: the queue is clogged with projects that are occupying space, consuming study resources, and driving up costs for the projects behind them -- and most of them will never get built.

This is not just an inconvenience. It's a direct threat to our ability to meet clean energy targets.

---

## The Math Doesn't Work for 2030 Targets

The US has ambitious clean energy goals. Multiple states have 100% clean energy standards. The federal government has targeted 80% clean electricity by 2030. Corporate PPAs are driving unprecedented demand for renewable energy.

But here's the uncomfortable truth: if the average project takes five years to get from application to operation, and the withdrawal rate is 80%, the pipeline of projects that will actually come online by 2030 is a fraction of what we need.

The LBNL data shows that only about 20% of projects entering the queue reach commercial operation. Of the 2,600+ GW in the queue, that suggests roughly 520 GW might actually get built -- eventually. But "eventually" and "by 2030" are very different things.

> We can't meet our clean energy targets if projects can't connect to the grid. It's that simple. The interconnection queue is now the binding constraint on the energy transition.

The irony is brutal. We have the technology. We have the economics. We have the demand. And we're stuck because the administrative process for connecting to the grid can't keep up.

---

## FERC Order 2023: The Biggest Reform in Decades

The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission recognized the crisis and issued Order 2023 in July 2023 -- the most significant reform to interconnection rules in twenty years.

The key changes:

- **Cluster studies instead of serial studies.** Instead of studying projects one at a time, grid operators now study groups of projects together. This is more efficient and produces more realistic results, since you're modeling a realistic set of projects rather than assuming every single application will get built.
- **Financial readiness requirements.** Higher deposits and commercial readiness demonstrations are designed to weed out speculative applications. You need to show you're serious -- with site control, financial backing, and development progress -- before you can enter the queue.
- **Withdrawal penalties.** If you withdraw from the queue after a certain point, you forfeit a portion of your deposit. This discourages the "apply everywhere, see what sticks" strategy.
- **Firm study timelines.** Grid operators are required to complete studies within defined timeframes, with consequences for missing deadlines.

These reforms are significant and directionally correct. But implementation is still rolling out across different regions, and the backlog of existing projects in the queue -- the so-called "transition queue" -- still needs to be processed under the old rules. It's going to take years before the full benefits of Order 2023 are felt.

---

## Regional Differences: Not All Queues Are Created Equal

One thing I've learned working across different markets is that the interconnection experience varies dramatically by region. Where you're developing matters as much as what you're developing.

**ERCOT (Texas).** Texas operates its own grid, independent of the two major US interconnections. ERCOT has historically been faster at processing interconnection requests, partly because it's a single-state system with fewer regulatory layers. But even ERCOT is feeling the pressure -- solar and storage applications have surged, and wait times are growing. The advantage? ERCOT's market structure and relatively streamlined process still make it one of the faster queues in the country.

**PJM (Mid-Atlantic and Midwest).** PJM has been one of the most congested queues in the US. In 2022, PJM essentially paused its queue to implement reforms, creating a massive backlog. The transition to cluster studies is underway, but developers in PJM territory have been dealing with some of the longest wait times in the country -- seven years or more in some cases. If you're developing in PJM, plan accordingly.

**CAISO (California).** California's queue reflects the state's aggressive clean energy goals. CAISO has been processing large volumes of solar and storage applications, and the queue is heavily weighted toward solar-plus-storage projects, reflecting the state's need for dispatchable capacity. Cluster studies have been in place longer here than in some other regions, but the sheer volume of applications keeps timelines long.

**MISO (Central US).** MISO implemented a reformed queue process and has been working through a massive backlog of projects, particularly wind and solar in the Upper Midwest. MISO's tranche-based approach studies projects in large batches, which can be efficient but also means that if your tranche gets delayed, everything moves together. MISO has also been more aggressive about assigning network upgrade costs, which has led to sticker shock for many developers.

**SPP (Southwest Power Pool).** SPP covers wind-rich territory across the Great Plains and has seen enormous volumes of wind and solar applications. Like other regions, SPP is transitioning to cluster studies and implementing stricter readiness requirements.

> The takeaway for developers: know your region. Study the specific queue dynamics, typical timelines, and cost allocation methodologies before you commit to a site. What works in ERCOT might be a nightmare in PJM.

---

## Solutions Being Tried -- and What's Actually Working

The industry isn't sitting still. Here's what I'm seeing in terms of solutions, and my honest assessment of each.

**Grid-enhancing technologies (GETs).** Dynamic line ratings, advanced power flow controllers, and topology optimization can squeeze more capacity out of existing transmission lines without building new ones. The DOE's Interconnection Innovation Exchange has been promoting these technologies, and they're genuinely promising. Dynamic line ratings alone can increase transmission capacity by 10-40% on existing lines. The problem is adoption -- utilities have been slow to deploy GETs at scale, partly because the regulatory framework doesn't always reward them for it.

**Co-located storage.** Adding battery storage to a generation project can help manage grid impact by smoothing output and reducing the need for transmission upgrades. Some developers are finding that co-locating storage actually reduces their assigned interconnection costs, because the combined project has a smaller grid impact than the generation project alone. This is a smart strategy that I'm seeing more developers adopt.

**Ready-to-build requirements.** By requiring developers to demonstrate site control, permitting progress, and financial commitments before entering the queue, regulators are trying to filter out speculative projects. This should improve completion rates over time, though it also raises the barrier to entry for smaller developers.

**Proactive transmission planning.** Instead of waiting for generators to request interconnection and then figuring out what transmission is needed, some regions are starting to plan transmission proactively -- identifying high-resource areas and building transmission to serve them before the projects show up. This is how it should work, but it requires long-term planning and upfront capital that doesn't have an immediate return.

**Fast-track processes for small projects.** Some regions are implementing expedited review processes for smaller projects (typically under 20 MW) that connect at distribution voltage. This won't solve the utility-scale problem, but it can unlock significant distributed generation capacity.

---

## The PM Perspective: How to Navigate This

If you're a project manager or developer working in clean energy right now, here's my honest advice on navigating the interconnection queue.

**Start early. Earlier than you think.** Interconnection should be one of the very first things you address in project development -- not an afterthought. Submit your application as early as possible, because the clock doesn't start until you're in the queue.

**Budget for delays.** Whatever timeline you're planning, add at least 18-24 months of contingency. I know that sounds painful, but I've seen too many projects get caught off guard by study delays, restudies, and queue reform transitions.

**Do your homework on upgrade costs.** Before you commit to a site, study the local transmission system. Are the substations and lines near capacity? Are there other projects ahead of you in the queue at the same point of interconnection? A site with cheap land and great solar resource is worthless if the interconnection costs are $50 million.

**Consider storage as a strategic tool.** Co-locating battery storage isn't just good for project economics -- it can meaningfully reduce your interconnection timeline and cost. Model it early.

**Engage with the process.** Attend stakeholder meetings. Submit comments on queue reform proposals. Build relationships with the transmission provider's interconnection team. This isn't just good citizenship -- it gives you intelligence on how the process is evolving and where the bottlenecks are.

**Have a portfolio strategy.** If you can afford it, develop multiple projects across multiple regions. Diversification is your hedge against any single project getting stuck in queue limbo.

> The interconnection queue rewards the patient and the prepared. If you go in expecting a smooth, predictable process, you're going to be disappointed. If you go in with realistic timelines, strong financials, and a willingness to adapt, you'll get projects built.

---

## The Bigger Picture

I genuinely believe we'll solve this problem. FERC Order 2023 is a real step forward. Grid-enhancing technologies are maturing. Proactive transmission planning is gaining traction. And the sheer economic pressure -- trillions of dollars of clean energy investment looking for a path to the grid -- will force the system to adapt.

But I also believe we need to be honest about the timeline. The queue crisis won't be resolved in a year or two. The reforms are rolling out gradually, the backlog is enormous, and new applications keep coming. Realistic expectation: meaningful improvement by 2027-2028, with the full benefits of reform felt by 2030.

In the meantime, every month that a viable clean energy project sits in queue limbo is a month of clean electrons that don't get generated. A month of fossil fuel generation that doesn't get displaced. A month of emissions that didn't need to happen.

The interconnection queue is an engineering problem, a regulatory problem, and a resource problem all wrapped into one. But more than anything, it's a problem of a system built for the old energy world trying to cope with the new one. We need to build the bridge between those two worlds faster.

If you're working in this space, I'd love to hear about your experience. What are you seeing in the queues? How are you adapting? The more we share what's working -- and what isn't -- the faster we all get through this.

---

**Sources:** Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory Queued Up Report 2025, LBNL Grid Connection Study 2025, FERC Order 2023, DOE Interconnection Innovation Exchange, US EIA, regional ISO/RTO interconnection data.
